items.title: Significance and Ranking of Heavy Metals in Toxicity
items.content: Heavy metals such as arsenic, lead, mercury, and cadmium are ranked highly by the U.S. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry due to their prevalence and severity of toxicity. These metals are also listed among the top 10 chemicals of highest concern globally by the World Health Organization.
keyPoints: Arsenic, lead, mercury, and cadmium are the first, second, third, and seventh hazards on the U.S. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry list.
These metals are also among the 10 chemicals of highest concern globally according to WHO.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms of Metal Toxicity
items.content: Metals can cause toxicity through various mechanisms including enzyme inhibition, damage to subcellular organelles and DNA, covalent modification of proteins, formation of reactive oxygen species, and displacement of essential metals in metal-dependent proteins. Recent research highlights their impact on DNA methylation and epigenetic changes.
keyPoints: Toxicity mechanisms include enzyme inhibition, damage to subcellular organelles/DNA, protein modification, reactive oxygen species formation, and displacement of essential metals.
Metals can affect DNA methylation and alter gene expression.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Metal Toxicity
items.content: The primary treatment for metal toxicity involves recognizing and terminating exposure. Chelating agents such as dimercaprol, EDTA, succimer, and penicillamine are used to bind metals into stable compounds with low toxicity, enhancing their excretion. These treatments are generally reserved for acute, symptomatic cases.
keyPoints: Chelating agents include dimercaprol (BAL), EDTA, succimer (DMSA), and penicillamine.
Activated charcoal is not effective for metal ingestion as it does not bind metals.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 1)

items.title: Arsenic Exposure and Its Health Implications
items.content: Arsenic exposure, even at moderate levels, is linked to increased risks of various cancers and cardiovascular diseases. The American Heart Association recognizes low-level arsenic as a risk factor for hypertension, subclinical atherosclerosis, coronary artery stenosis, ischemic heart disease, stroke, left ventricular hypertrophy, heart failure, and peripheral artery disease. Arsenic exposure is also associated with lung function impairment, respiratory infections, nonmalignant lung disease mortality, neurodevelopmental delays in children, type 2 diabetes, and liver diseases.
keyPoints: Increased risks for skin, bladder, renal pelvis, ureter, kidney, liver, and lung cancer
Modified by smoking, folate and selenium status, genetic traits
Risk factors for hypertension, subclinical atherosclerosis, coronary artery stenosis, ischemic heart disease, stroke, left ventricular hypertrophy, heart failure, peripheral artery disease
Evidence of arsenic exposure as a risk factor for lung function impairment, respiratory infections, nonmalignant lung disease mortality
Association with cardiovascular disease at levels below WHO guideline value of 10 μg/L
Likely cause of neurodevelopmental delays in children, type 2 diabetes, nonalcoholic fatty liver disease, and cirrhosis
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 2)

items.title: Cadmium Exposure and Its Health Implications
items.content: Cadmium exposure is linked to bone toxicity, lower bone density, higher incidence of fractures, decline in height, kidney graft failure, cardiovascular disease, and increased mortality from respiratory infections. Cadmium is classified as a known carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer, with elevated risks for prostate, lung, breast, and endometrial cancer.
keyPoints: 1946 outbreak of 'itai-itai' disease due to cadmium-induced bone toxicity
Associated with lower bone density, higher incidence of fractures, decline in height
Increased risk of long-term kidney graft failure
Synergy between cadmium and lead on kidney function
Linked to lower lung function, cardiovascular disease, stroke, heart failure
Higher mortality from influenza, pneumonia, COVID-19
Classified as a known carcinogen with elevated risks for prostate, lung, breast, endometrial cancer
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 2)

items.title: Lead Toxicity and Its Health Implications
items.content: Advances in measuring cumulative lead exposure through x-ray fluorescence have revealed its association with chronic diseases. Higher bone lead levels are linked to increased risk of hypertension and accelerated declines in health, despite declining blood lead levels due to regulatory measures.
keyPoints: Development of KXRF instruments for safe in vivo measurements of bone lead
Cumulative lead exposure as a risk factor for chronic disease
Blood lead levels declined but still present at 1–2 μg/dL range
Higher bone lead levels linked to increased risk of hypertension and accelerated health declines
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 2)

items.question: Which heavy metal is ranked as the first hazard by the U.S. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry?
items.explanation: Arsenic is listed as the first hazard on the list of heavy metals.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 1)

items.question: What is a common route through which metals are inhaled?
items.explanation: Metals are primarily inhaled as dusts and fumes.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following metals is essential to normal metabolic function but toxic at high levels?
items.explanation: Copper and selenium are essential trace elements that can be toxic at high levels.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 1)

items.question: Which chelating agent is known by the name British anti-Lewisite?
items.explanation: Dimercaprol is also known as British anti-Lewisite (BAL).
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 1)

items.question: Which heavy metal is associated with 'itai-itai' disease due to its bone toxicity?
items.explanation: Itai-itai disease, characterized by painful bone fractures, was caused by cadmium poisoning.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 2)

items.question: Which heavy metal exposure is linked to cardiovascular diseases at levels below the WHO guideline value?
items.explanation: Arsenic exposure has been associated with cardiovascular diseases even at levels below the WHO guideline.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 2)

items.question: Which heavy metal is classified as a known carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer?
items.explanation: The International Agency for Research on Cancer has classified cadmium as a known carcinogen.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 2)

items.question: What recent advancement aids in measuring cumulative lead exposure?
items.explanation: KXRF instruments are used for safe in vivo measurements of lead levels in bone, reflecting cumulative exposure.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 2)

items.statement: Activated charcoal is effective in treating acute metal ingestion.
items.explanation: Activated charcoal does not bind metals and is of limited usefulness for acute metal ingestion.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 1)

items.statement: Genetic factors can modify the impact of metals on health.
items.explanation: Polymorphisms that encode variant enzymes may alter metal binding, transport, and effects.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 1)

items.statement: Cadmium is not associated with any cardiovascular diseases.
items.explanation: The American Heart Association considers cadmium a risk factor for cardiovascular disease and mortality.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 2)

items.statement: Lead exposure is no longer a concern due to its removal from gasoline and plumbing.
items.explanation: Despite the decline in blood lead levels, higher cumulative lead exposure remains a risk factor for chronic disease.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 2)

items.assertion: The principal chelating agents are reserved for treating acute, symptomatic metal toxicity.
items.reason: Chelating agents bind metals into stable compounds with low toxicity to enhance excretion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason explains why chelating agents are used in acute cases.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 1)

items.assertion: Metals can cause toxicity through the inhibition of enzymes.
items.reason: Metals affect DNA methylation and other changes to the epigenome, altering gene expression.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion about enzyme inhibition.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 1)

items.assertion: Arsenic exposure is linked to lung function impairment.
items.reason: Recent studies have shown arsenic as a risk factor for respiratory symptoms and nonmalignant lung disease mortality.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 2)

items.assertion: Cadmium exposure is only harmful at very high levels.
items.reason: Environmental exposures to cadmium have been linked to various health issues even at modest levels.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; cadmium can be harmful at modest environmental exposure levels.
items.reference: Poisoning Drug Overdose and Envenomation · Heavy Metal Poisoning Howard Hu (part 2)
